FederatedAnalysisDataSHIELD 1.0.0
  • Reference
    • Connecting to the federation
    • Extracting a tailored analysis dataset
    • Federated regression on an extracted table

Federated regression on an extracted table

Source: vignettes/03_modeling.Rmd
03_modeling.Rmd

Every result on this page was produced by executing the code against the live three-site federation (repository steps 1–4). Connection parameters are read from the gitignored sites.env.

Because a dsOMOP extract is just an ordinary server-side data frame, the whole dsBaseClient modelling toolchain applies to it directly. And because dsOMOP harmonises sex into a federation-aligned factor — coded identically on every site, as the previous page showed — it is model-ready as-is: ds.glm builds the contrast for us, so the old manual 0/1 recoding is no longer needed. We fit the same federated GLM two ways — pooled individual-level and study-level meta-analysis — then a logistic model the other direction. Only the models’ sufficient statistics are pooled across the three sites; no patient row ever leaves a server.

Log in, attach, and re-extract the demographics table 

library(DSI); library(DSOpal); library(dsBaseClient); library(dsOMOPClient)

find_up <- function(f, d = getwd()) {
  repeat {
    p <- file.path(d, f); if (file.exists(p)) return(p)
    up <- dirname(d); if (identical(up, d)) stop(f, " not found"); d <- up
  }
}
read_env <- function(path) {
  ln <- trimws(readLines(path, warn = FALSE))
  ln <- ln[nzchar(ln) & !startsWith(ln, "#")]
  e <- list()
  for (kv in regmatches(ln, regexec("^([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)=(.*)$", ln)))
    if (length(kv) == 3L) e[[kv[2]]] <- kv[3]
  e
}
cfg <- read_env(Sys.getenv("SITES_ENV", unset = find_up("sites.env")))

builder <- DSI::newDSLoginBuilder()
for (S in sub("_OPAL_URL$", "", grep("_OPAL_URL$", names(cfg), value = TRUE)))
  builder$append(server = tolower(S), url = cfg[[paste0(S, "_OPAL_URL")]],
                 user = cfg$OPAL_USER, password = cfg$OPAL_PASSWORD,
                 resource = cfg$OPAL_RESOURCE_PATH,
                 driver = "OpalDriver", profile = cfg$OPAL_PROFILE)
conns <- DSI::datashield.login(logins = builder$build())
ds.omop.connect(resource = cfg$OPAL_RESOURCE_PATH, symbol = "omop", conns = conns)

plan <- ds.omop.plan()
plan <- ds.omop.plan.person_level(
  plan,
  tables = list(person = c(sex = "gender_concept_id", birth_year = "year_of_birth")),
  name = "demographics")
plan <- ds.omop.plan.options(plan, factor_concepts = TRUE)   # on by default
ds.omop.plan.execute(plan, out = c(demographics = "D"), symbol = "omop", conns = conns)

Linear model: birth year by sex

sex is a factor coded identically on every site, so we regress on it directly — ds.glm constructs the contrast itself. The reference level is 8507 (male, the lowest concept id), so in birth_year ~ sex the intercept is the mean birth year of men and the sex8532 term is the women-minus-men difference:

g <- ds.glm(formula = "D$birth_year ~ D$sex", family = "gaussian",
            datasources = conns)
g$coefficients
#>                 Estimate Std. Error      z-value   p-value   low0.95CI
#> (Intercept) 1958.2899319  0.4447214 4403.4080884 0.0000000 1957.418294
#> D$sex8532     -0.4385116  0.6229477   -0.7039301 0.4814763   -1.659467
#>               high0.95CI
#> (Intercept) 1959.1615699
#> D$sex8532      0.7824434

Meta-analysis: the same model, study-level

ds.glm above pools individual-level sufficient statistics — one model fitted over the combined federation. ds.glmSLMA instead fits the model separately on each site and meta-combines the per-site estimates (study-level meta-analysis), sharing no individual-level statistic across sites. On a harmonised factor the two routes agree:

gs <- ds.glmSLMA(formula = "D$birth_year ~ D$sex", family = "gaussian",
                 datasources = conns)
gs$SLMA.pooled.ests.matrix
#>                pooled.ML     se.ML  pooled.REML   se.REML    pooled.FE
#> (Intercept) 1958.3250039 0.4437898 1958.3243573 0.4486745 1958.3250041
#> D$sex8532     -0.4127396 0.6220430   -0.4127396 0.6220430   -0.4127396
#>                 se.FE
#> (Intercept) 0.4437888
#> D$sex8532   0.6220430

Logistic model: sex by birth year

The harmonised factor is equally usable as a binomial response. Modelling sex ~ birth_year estimates the log-odds of being female (8532, the non-reference level) as a function of birth year:

gb <- ds.glm(formula = "D$sex ~ D$birth_year", family = "binomial",
             datasources = conns)
gb$coefficients
#>                 Estimate  Std. Error    z-value   p-value low0.95CI.LP
#> (Intercept)   3.32848244 4.672977755  0.7122830 0.4762896  -5.83038566
#> D$birth_year -0.00168016 0.002386432 -0.7040469 0.4814036  -0.00635748
#>              high0.95CI.LP      P_OR low0.95CI.P_OR high0.95CI.P_OR
#> (Intercept)    12.48735054 0.9653931    0.002928344       0.9999962
#> D$birth_year    0.00299716 0.9983213    0.993662686       1.0030017

Disconnect 

ds.omop.disconnect(symbol = "omop", conns = conns)
DSI::datashield.logout(conns)

The models above are standard dsBaseClient fits computed on a dsOMOP extract: the OMOP layer produced and harmonised the analysis dataset — including the factor coding that made sex model-ready across the federation — and the ordinary federated toolchain took it from there.

On this page

Developed by David Sarrat González, Michael Ochola.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
